perturbation.original_text: 1.01 Definitions Definitions can be found at URL: http://www.cpr.ca/en/about -cp/selling -to-cp/information -technology/definitions
perturbation.changed_text: 1.01 Definitions Definitions related to this CNDA are outlined in the sections below.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the URL where the definitions are located and stating that definitions are outlined in the 'sections below', and no definitions are actually defined in those subsequent sections, this creates an in-text contradiction because other sections rely on these definitions. The sections that will be impacted by this are: 2.01 Confidential Information, 2.02 Not Confidential Information, and 3.11 Residuals.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.01 Confidential Information "Confidential Information " means, subject to section 2.02 below any additional information requested by the Supplier and provided by CPR , or any information supplied by either party that is clearly marked “Confidential” or clearly intended to be disclosed in confidence.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a URL where definitions could be found. The changed text states that definitions are outlined in the sections below, but according to the explanation, no definitions are actually defined in those sections. The contradicted text (2.01 Confidential Information) relies on the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Because the definitions are not actually included, and the location of these definitions are removed by the changed text, this creates a strong contradiction because the definition relied upon in section 2.01 is effectively removed, impacting its enforceability and creating uncertainty. This aligns with the definition of Omissions – In Text Contradiction: 'Omissions occur when essential language is removed or altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.'

perturbation.original_text: 2.01 Confidential Information "Confidential Information " means, subject to section 2.02 below any additional information requested by the Supplier and provided by CPR , or any information supplied by either party that is clearly marked “Confidential” or clearly intended to be disclosed in confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.01 Confidential Information Subject to section 2.02 below, information is deemed confidential at the sole discretion of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is now changed to a broad, subjective criteria. This directly contradicts the more specific exclusions listed in section 2.02, which outlines specific types of information that are *not* considered confidential, regardless of whether the disclosing party deems them so. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.02 Not Confidential Information The following shall not, for the purposes of this CNDA, constitute Confidential Information: (a) information relating to the Disclosing Party that is obtained or was previously obtained by the Receiving Party from a thir d Person who, insofar as is known to the Receiving Party after reasonable inquiry, is not obligated to keep such information confidential; (b) information already known to the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure, as shown by prior written evidence o r other evidence satisfactory to the Disclosing Party; (c) information that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party’s violation of this CNDA; and (d) information that the Disclosing Part y authorizes the Receiving Party to disclose.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to the 'sole discretion of the disclosing party.' This directly contradicts section 2.02, which lists specific types of information that are *not* considered confidential, regardless of the disclosing party's opinion. The change makes the application of section 2.02 unclear, creating a contradiction because the discretion granted in the changed text conflicts with the specific exceptions listed in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 2.03 Kept in Confidence The Receiving Party agrees to use the same degree of care to prevent disclosure of the Confidential Information received by it as the Receiving Party uses to prevent disclosure of its own confidential information, but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care. In addition, except as set forth in section 2.06, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, neither the Receiving Party nor its Representatives will disclo se to any other Person the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party, or any of the terms, conditions or other facts relating to the Parties’ business relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.03 Kept in Confidence The Receiving Party agrees to use reasonable efforts to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information. In addition, except as set forth in section 2.06, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, neither the Receiving Party nor its Representatives will disclose to any other Person the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party, or any of the terms, conditions or other facts relating to the Parties’ business relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the same degree of care to prevent disclosure of the Confidential Information received by it as the Receiving Party uses to prevent disclosure of its own confidential information, but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'reasonable efforts' weakens the protection offered to the disclosing party. This contradicts the clause 3.01 (Remedies), because the 'irreparable harm' it claims to prevent is now less protected. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.01 Remedies The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that it has entered into this CNDA on the understanding that any breach hereof by it will cause the Disclosing Party irreparable harm and expressly agrees that, in addition to all other remedies that the Disclosi ng Party may be entitled to as a matter of law, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specific performance and any form of equitable relief to enforce the provisions of this CNDA.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 'degree of care' for protecting confidential information, with a 'reasonable degree of care' as the minimum. The changed text weakens this protection to 'reasonable efforts'. Clause 3.01 states that any breach will cause 'irreparable harm'. By weakening the protection of confidential information, the change increases the likelihood of a breach, directly contradicting the assumption in 3.01 that such harm is preventable with the original higher standard of care. This creates a strong contradiction because the remedies are based on a level of protection that has been lessened.

perturbation.original_text: 2.07 Return of Confidential Information Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall promptly ret urn to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information in its possession or control, and promptly destroy or erase all notes, memoranda and other material prepared by the Receiving Party which reflect, interpret, evaluate, include or are derived from any Confidential Information and the Receiving Party shall certify such destruction in writing to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.07 Return of Confidential Information Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall return accessible Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to destroy notes, memoranda, and other derivative materials, and the requirement to certify destruction, the changed text allows the receiving party to retain copies and analyses of the Confidential Information. This creates an in-text contradiction with section 2.04 (No Benefit, Restricted Use), which prohibits the Receiving Party from deriving any benefit from the Confidential Information because the party is in fact retaining the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.04 No Benefit, Restricte d Use The Receiving Party shall not, in any manner, derive any benefit, directly or indirectly, from the Confidential Information or the use of such Confidential Information, for any purpose. The Receiving Party agrees not to appropriate for his or her ow n use or exploit in any way whatsoever any of the Confidential Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation to destroy notes and derivative materials of confidential information, and also removes the certification requirement. This directly contradicts section 2.04, which prohibits the Receiving Party from deriving any benefit from the Confidential Information, as retaining copies and analyses allows the receiving party to potentially derive a benefit, creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 3.09 Termination This agreement continues in effect for three years. Either party may terminate t his CNDA for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice . The termination of this CNDA , or the completion or abandonment of the Permitted Purpose, shall not affect the survival of the non -disclosure obligations under this CNDA , which obligations shall survive for a period o f 3 years .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.09 Termination This agreement continues in effect for three years. Either party may terminate this CNDA for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that the non-disclosure obligations survive termination contradicts the purpose of the entire agreement. This contradicts the survival clause by removing the crucial caveat that the confidentiality obligations continue after termination, undermining the main goal of the CNDA and it directly contradicts 3.02.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.02 Continuing Obligations Regarding Confidentiality The obligations of confidentiality contained herein shall survive the expiration or termination of the Contract, SOW, Amendments, or RFP Terms.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the crucial clause stating that non-disclosure obligations survive termination. This directly contradicts section 3.02, which explicitly states that confidentiality obligations *shall* survive expiration or termination. The omission undermines the core purpose of a CNDA, creating a strong in-text contradiction that makes the contract inconsistent and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3.11 Residuals Notwithstanding the confidentiality obligations contained in Article 2 hereof, the receiving party may, during and after the term hereof, use in its business any Residual Information. “Residual Information” means the ideas, know -how and techniques that would be retained in the unaided memory of an ordinary person skilled in the art, not CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON -DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT - MUTUAL 6 | P a g e Confidentiality and Non -Disclosure Agreement – version 1 80314 intent on appropriating the proprietary information of the disclosing party, as a result of such person’s access to, use, review, evaluation, or testing of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party for the purposes described herein. An employee's memory is unaided if the employee has not intentionally memorized the confidential information for the purpose of retaining and subsequently using or disclosing it . Nothin g in this paragraph, however, shall be deemed to grant to the receiving party a license under the disclosing party’s intellectual property rights
perturbation.changed_text: 3.11 Residuals The receiving party may use Residual Information. Nothing in this paragraph, however, shall be deemed to grant to the receiving party a license under the disclosing party’s intellectual property rights
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of Residual Information the text is now contradicting the clause since its undefined, and now it may lead to the receiving party using confidential information by saying it is residual information. The definition of Residual Information is required to know what this definition is in the document to begin with.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.11 Residuals The receiving party may use Residual Information. Nothing in this paragraph, however, shall be deemed to grant to the receiving party a license under the disclosing party’s intellectual property rights
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Residual Information'. The changed text removes this definition, while still referencing 'Residual Information'. Since the text defines that the receiving party may use Residual Information, and the definition of that term is removed from the contract, that creates a contradiction. The removal creates a risk that the receiving party may use confidential information, since the definition of what Residual Information actually is, is removed. This aligns with the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition provided.
